conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child - Alex, who is 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To continue assessing your eligibility, I need to ask:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great. Next, I need to understand your living situation with Alex:

Does Alex normally live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. This is an important detail. Can you tell me who Alex normally lives with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask:

Do you contribute at least the same amount as the other parent towards Alex's upkeep, or are you the main financial contributor?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't really contribute financially at the moment - I haven't been able to help with their upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about your situation. Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. This is usually the person the child lives with. In situations where the child lives with someone else, you might still be able to claim if you contribute at least as much towards the child's upkeep as the person they live with.

In your case:
- Alex lives with their other parent, not with you
- You're not currently contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep

Because you don't meet either of these criteria, you wouldn't be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. The other parent who Alex lives with would be the person eligible to claim.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you or if you begin contributing equally or more to their upkeep - your eligibility could change.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: According to UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. Generally, this is the person the child normally lives with. In cases where the child lives with someone else, you may still be eligible if you contribute at least the same amount to the child's upkeep as the person they live with. In this situation: (1) Alex normally lives with their other parent, not with you; (2) You are not contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep; (3) Therefore, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex as you do not meet the requirement of being the person the child lives with, nor do you contribute at least as much as the parent Alex lives with towards their upkeep.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has a 6-year-old child (Alex) who lives in the UK. However, Alex does not live with the claimant but instead lives with their other parent. The claimant does not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for each child - typically the person the child lives with. Since the claimant neither lives with Alex nor contributes at least equally to their upkeep, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
